content.problemStatement:
**This is the hard version of the problem. In this version, you need to answer for each index iii whether a maximal sequence of parries including that index exists.**

Chef is playing a videogame, and fighting a particularly difficult boss.

After several tries, Chef has learned the attack patterns of the boss and knows exactly what to expect.

The boss will perform NNN attacks, in order. Each attack can be either dodged or parried.

The skill level required to dodge the iii-th attack is AiA_iAi‚Äã, and the skill level required to parry the iii-th attack is BiB_iBi‚Äã.

Dodging is easier than parrying, so Ai‚â§BiA_i \leq B_iAi‚Äã‚â§Bi‚Äã holds for all iii.

Chef's initial skill level is XXX. The fight will proceed as follows:


The boss will use each of its attacks in order from 111 to NNN.
For the iii-th attack:

If X<AiX \lt A_iX<Ai‚Äã, Chef will get hit by the iii-th attack and lose the fight.
If Ai‚â§X<BiA_i \leq X \lt B_iAi‚Äã‚â§X<Bi‚Äã, Chef can only dodge the attack. XXX does not change.
If X‚â•BiX \geq B_iX‚â•Bi‚Äã, Chef can choose to either dodge or parry the attack.

If the attack is parried, XXX will decrease by 111. If the attack is dodged, XXX will not change.


Chef wins the fight if he manages to either dodge or parry every attack.

Chef finds parrying much cooler than dodging, so he wants to parry as many attacks as possible.

Let MMM denote the maximum number of attacks Chef can parry, while also ensuring that he wins the fight.

For each iii from 111 to NNN, answer the following question independently:


Is it possible to parry MMM attacks in total, including attack iii, and still win the fight?

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of three lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and XXX ‚Äî the number of attacks and Chef's initial skill level.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã ‚Äì the skill levels of dodging each attack.
The third line contains NNN space-separated integers B1,B2,‚Ä¶,BNB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_NB1‚Äã,B2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,BN‚Äã ‚Äì the skill levels of parrying each attack.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line a binary string of length NNN.

The iii-th character should be 111 if MMM attacks can be parried including the iii-th, and 000 otherwise.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2‚ãÖ1052\cdot 10^52‚ãÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: It's not possible to parry all four attacks, since parrying the first two attacks will result in XXX becoming 999, so the third cannot be parried.
However, note that attacks 1,2,41, 2, 41,2,4 can be parried without issue, so we have M=3M = 3M=3.
Now,

Attacks 1,2,41, 2, 41,2,4 can be parried as noted above.
Another valid sequence of length 333 is to parry attacks 2,3,42, 3, 42,3,4.

So, for each index, there's a maximal sequence that allows us to parry it.
Test case 222: Almost the same as the first test, but with X=10X = 10X=10 now.
The value of MMM is still 333 since attacks 1,2,41, 2, 41,2,4 can be parried.
However, there's no way to parry M=3M = 3M=3 attacks while also parrying attack 333. This is because if either of the first two attacks are parried, XXX will fall below B3=10B_3 = 10B3‚Äã=10; but if both of them are not parried then it's impossible to reach 333 parries in total.
Test case 333: We have no choice but to dodge all attacks. M=0M = 0M=0 here.
Test case 444: M=1M = 1M=1, and only the last attack can be parried.
The first attack can't be parried even though XXX is large enough, since it will result in being unable to dodge the second attack.
